title: Ryan Gander: Art should make you uncomfortable
gist: Artist Ryan Gander, coordinating the Royal Academy's 2026 Summer Exhibition (a record 1,800+ works across 258 years), argues that personal 'liking' is irrelevant to artistic quality and that art's real value lies in ephemeral effects — memories, stories, discomfort — rather than in pleasing objects. He recounts being rejected without interview by every major UK art school he applied to, and now teaches at several of them.

insights.claim: Ryan Gander argues that whether a viewer personally 'likes' an artwork says nothing about its quality, comparing it to preferring Chinese food over pizza without pizza being bad.
insights.speaker: Ryan Gander
insights.so_what: Reframes how critics and institutions should evaluate art away from popularity/likability metrics — relevant to how galleries position work for broad audiences without chasing crowd-pleasing 'likability.'
insights.quote: We use ‘like’ as if that means something. Whether you like it doesn’t mean it’s a good artwork or not.
insights.locator: We ask the wrong questions about art

insights.claim: Gander distinguishes 'art for the eyes' from 'art for the brain,' arguing the real value of art lies in ephemeral, non-material effects — memories, stories, relationships — rather than in the physical object itself.
insights.speaker: Ryan Gander
insights.so_what: Signals a curatorial ethos privileging conceptual/experiential art over decorative, object-driven work, shaping how the RA's flagship annual show is positioned relative to more commercial art fairs.
insights.quote: we should be obsessed with ephemeral things like memories, stories, relationships.
insights.locator: Gander tells me that he makes a keen distinction

insights.claim: Gander suggests that art which sticks in a viewer's head long after the fact may not properly be classified as 'bad,' inverting the usual assumption that lingering dislike signals artistic failure.
insights.speaker: Ryan Gander
insights.so_what: Reframes negative or controversial critical reception as a possible proxy for cultural staying power — a useful lens for assessing buzz-driven, polarizing contemporary art over a longer horizon.

insights.claim: The 2026 Royal Academy Summer Exhibition, coordinated by Gander around the theme of 'interconnectedness,' brings together more than 1,800 works, a new record in the show's 258-year history.
insights.speaker: David McAllister
insights.so_what: Signals rising scale and public engagement for the RA's flagship, revenue-generating exhibition — a proxy for the health of the RA's brand and the broader UK institutional art market.
insights.quote: a new record in the show’s 258-year history

insights.claim: Gander's display features a pale line running through every room at two-metre height, referencing the historical convention that art hung above a gentleman's top-hat height signalled higher prestige, while hanging work deliberately too high to see ('skying') was historically a mark of disrespect at the RA.
insights.speaker: Ryan Gander
insights.so_what: Shows an institutional insider embedding art-historical hierarchy critique directly into a major exhibition's physical design, rather than only in wall text or catalogue framing.
insights.locator: except for a pale line running through every room

insights.claim: Gander says most contemporary art is now made to be 'pleasing' and comfortable because the world feels frightening, but insists a worthwhile exhibition should challenge and unsettle viewers rather than please them.
insights.speaker: Ryan Gander
insights.so_what: Positions this year's RA Summer Exhibition against the broader trend toward comfort-oriented, market-friendly contemporary art — worth watching whether visitor and critical reception rewards or punishes a discomfort-first curatorial bet.
insights.quote: I think a lot of art now is made to be pleasing, to make you feel comfortable. I understand that, because the world’s scary as fuck.
insights.locator: He hopes that what makes his display unique

insights.claim: Gander was rejected without even an interview by every major UK art school he applied to as a young artist, including Goldsmiths, Central Saint Martins, Chelsea College of Arts and the Royal College of Art; those same institutions now invite him to teach.
insights.speaker: Ryan Gander
insights.so_what: Illustrates how weakly elite UK art-school admissions predict later institutional success — a data point on the limits of academic gatekeeping as a talent filter in the art world.
insights.quote: Never got in, never even got an interview at any of them. Now they ask me to teach there,
insights.locator: I have my experience of applying to things like this
